mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M113K engine typically costs £4,500–£8,500 supply only, with used units available from £1,800–£4,000. Rebuilt engines from specialist suppliers start at around £6,995. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your G55.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the M113K is widely regarded as 'pretty much bulletproof in stock form' and 'one of the most robust German performance engines'. Examples have been documented exceeding 246,000 miles. The main issues are the intercooler pump (a common wear item), oil leaks, and timing chain tensioner wear at high mileage. In stock form, it's one of the most reliable AMG V8s ever built.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG?

acceptedAnswer.text: Intercooler pump failure (causes supercharger shutoff after hard acceleration — replace with upgraded Bosch A000 500 03 86 or Johnson CM30 pump), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and oil cooler seals, timing chain tensioner wear at higher mileages, and supercharger clutch 'chirp'. The engine is fundamentally robust.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: G55 AMG values remain strong — a well-kept example is often worth £20,000–£50,000+. A £4,500–£8,500 reconditioned engine is 10–30% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example, replacement is almost always worthwhile. The G55's iconic status and rising values make engine replacement a sound investment.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is M113K (M113.982) — it's the only 5.4-litre supercharged V8 used in the G55 AMG. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself. Production ran from 2004 to 2011.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M113K timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,500–£3,500 and is a labour-intensive job. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,500+). At the first sign of rattling or slapping from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current G55 AMG market. Reconditioned engines from specialist suppliers start at around £6,995 for rebuilds, with fully reconditioned units typically ranging from £4,500–£8,500. The M113K is relatively affordable to replace compared to newer AMG V8s, thanks to its widespread use across multiple models and strong aftermarket support.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G55 AMG engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The G-Wagen's body-on-frame construction makes engine access surprisingly straightforward compared to other AMG models.

cta.label: → Compare G55 AMG engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Intercooler Pump Failure — The "Supercharger Shutoff"
problems.symptoms: Huge loss of power after 1–2 hard accelerations, supercharger shuts off for 30 seconds to 1 minute, sluggish performance, feeling of reduced power
problems.recommendation: The intercooler circulation pump is the single most common failure on the M113K. The original pumps fail frequently — many owners are on their third pump. The pump is responsible for circulating coolant through the intercoolers to keep intake air temperatures down. When it fails, the ECU detects rising intake temperatures and shuts off the supercharger to protect the engine. The upgraded Bosch pump (part number A000 500 03 86) is the recommended replacement. Many owners also upgrade to the Johnson CM30 pump for improved reliability and cooling performance.

failureLink.label: Read the full intercooler pump failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover Gaskets and Oil Cooler Seals
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, visible oil residue on the engine block, low oil level warning
problems.recommendation: The M113K is known for oil leaks, particularly from valve cover gaskets, the oil cooler gasket, and the oil pressure test port. These leaks are rarely catastrophic but should be addressed to prevent oil loss and potential fire hazards. Replacing valve cover gaskets is a routine job for any Mercedes specialist. Check for oil residue around the valve covers regularly and investigate any unexplained oil consumption.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner and Guide Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling or slapping noise from the front of the engine, especially on cold start or acceleration, metallic ticking
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£8,500 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)
problems.recommendation: While the M113K's timing chain is generally robust, it can stretch over time. The tensioner is a known wear item. If the chain jumps, piston-valve contact is likely, destroying the engine. One owner reported that a failed $1 rubber o-ring on the front timing cover after a water pump replacement allowed coolant to mix with oil, causing engine failure — a "rare show stopper that ruins motors". If you hear rattling from the front of the engine, have the timing chain inspected immediately. A preventative tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: G55 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple replacement — the most common M113K issue
Well-understood job — catch before oil starvation
Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Viable on a cherished example; rare on the M113K

buyingChecks.title: Buying This G55 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Evidence of intercooler pump replacement (upgraded Bosch or Johnson pump) — the most common failure addressed
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 5,000–10,000 miles) — the M113K rewards good maintenance
* No rattling from the front of the engine — timing chain tensioner should be healthy
* A well-maintained example — the M113K is "pretty much bulletproof in stock form"
avoidIf: * Any rattling or slapping from the front of the engine — timing chain tensioner wear is likely
* Evidence of repeated coolant top-ups without a diagnosed leak — head gasket or cooling system issues
* Poor service history — the M113K is robust but not invincible
* Modified examples without supporting engine work — modified M113Ks have been known to "blow up"

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M113K is one of the most robust and characterful engines AMG ever built — a "bulletproof", hand-built 5.4-litre supercharged V8 that delivers old-school AMG charm in a boxy off-road icon. Its weaknesses are well-understood: the intercooler pump (replace with the upgraded unit), oil leaks from valve covers and oil cooler seals, and timing chain wear at high mileage. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the G55 AMG will reward you with one of the most charismatic driving experiences of any vehicle ever made. It is "one of the most robust German performance engines" and, when stock, "pretty much bulletproof".

cta.label: → Compare G55 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz G55 AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Intercooler Pump — Any history of replacement? (the most common M113K failure — check for upgraded Bosch A000 500 03 86 or Johnson CM30 pump)
2. Cold Start — Listen for rattles or slapping from the front of the engine (timing chain tensioner warning)
3. Service History — including evidence of regular oil changes and major maintenance
4. Oil Leaks — Any oil under the car or around the valve covers? (common M113K issue)
5. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (head gasket or cooling system warning)
6. Supercharger Clutch — Any "chirp" noise? (clutch pack wear — common and harmless but indicates wear)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
